person.name: James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas
person.alternateName: 7th Earl of Douglas James "The Gross" Douglas
person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
James Douglas, 7th Earl of Douglas, 1st Earl of Avondale (1371-24 March 1443), latterly known as James the Gross, and prior to his ennoblement as James of Balvenie, was a late mediaeval Scottish magnate. He was the second son of Archibald Douglas, 3rd Earl of Douglas, and Joan Moray of Bothwell and Drumsargard (now Cambuslang), d. after 1408.

«b»Regent of Douglas«/b»
His rise to dominance in the kingdom began with the disastrous defeat of his elder brother Archibald Douglas, 4th Earl of Douglas, at the Battle of Homildon Hill in September 1402, where he was captured by the English. James was now acting head of the main branch of the powerful Douglas family and was left to maintain their influence in southern Scotland. His successes in this regard preserved Douglas influence until the return of his elder brother from captivity in 1409, at which time he assumed the role of councillor and was rewarded with the grant of extensive estates. The most important of these was the stronghold of Abercorn Castle, which would become his principal residence for the rest of his life.

«b»Relationship with the Stewarts«/b»
In the early 1420s James acted as the link between his brother, the earl, and the powerful Murdoch Stewart, Duke of Albany. Murdoch was acting as regent for his cousin, James I of Scotland, while James was being held by the English for ransom. Murdoch's links with the Albany Stewarts however, did not prevent him from becoming a councillor to the king once he returned from imprisonment in 1424. Along with his nephew, Archibald Douglas, 5th Earl of Douglas, he sat on the jury which convicted Murdoch and two of his sons of treason for failure to pay the ransom and allowing the king to be imprisoned for 18 years. In 1426 as a reward for his loyalty Douglas received royal confirmation of his lands and his eldest son William was knighted in 1430. By 1435 he was made sheriff of Lanarkshire, further strengthening his place among the king's principal followers. This backing from the Earl of Douglas' senior kinsman was vital to the king for preventing a clash with the powerful Douglas affinity. Shortly before James' death in 1437 James I gave him the title of Earl of Avondale.

«b»Earl of Douglas«/b»
When King James was assassinated in 1437 Douglas backed his nephew who became Lieutenant General of Scotland and Regent for the young James II until the death of his nephew, the 5th Earl, in 1439 of a fever, set off a deadly chain of events that saw power being uneasily shared between Sir William Crichton, Sir Alexander Livingston of Callendar and Douglas himself.

These events culminated in what would become known as the 'Black Dinner' at Edinburgh Castle, where his great-nephew William Douglas, 6th Earl of Douglas, 16 at the time, and his younger brother David were summarily beheaded on trumped up charges in the presence of a protesting young James II. Historians tend to place the blame for the Black Dinner on Crichton and Livingston, but especially Douglas, as the death of his great-nephews brought him the earldom of Douglas and the position as the most powerful magnate in Scotland.

«b»Marriage and issue«/b»
He married first Beatrice Stewart, daughter of Robert Stewart, 1st Duke of Albany and Margaret Graham, Countess of Menteith. He married second Beatrice Sinclair, daughter of Henry Sinclair, 2nd Earl of Orkney, around 1425.

1.) Beatrice Douglas, married William Hay, 1st Earl of Erroll, Lord High Constable of Scotland.

2.) William Douglas, 8th Earl of Douglas (1425-1452), who succeeded his father

3.) James Douglas, 9th Earl of Douglas (1426-1491), who succeeded his older brother

4.) Archibald Douglas, Earl of Moray (1426-1455), James's twin

5.) Hugh Douglas, Earl of Ormonde (d. 1455)

6.) John Douglas, Lord of Balvenie

7.) Janet Douglas, had a child with Constantine Dunlop, married Robert Fleming, 1st Lord Fleming

8.) Margaret Douglas, married Henry Douglas of Borg (d. 1473)

sourceList.title: Our Royal, Titled, Noble, and Commoner Ancestors & Cousins

zh.lifeSummary: 當James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas於1371年出生在蘇格蘭藍納可爾道格拉斯 卡素時，他的父親Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwell46歲，母親Joanna Moray Countess of Douglas40歲。他於1425年與Beatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglas在蘇格蘭阿吉爾奧陳布雷可結婚。他們至少是5個兒子和3個女兒的父母。他去世於1443年在英國蘇格蘭藍納可爾道格拉斯 卡素享年72歲，埋葬在蘇格蘭藍納可爾道格拉斯。

nameMeanings.name: Douglas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish: habitational name from any of various places called from their situation on a river named with Gaelic dubh ‘dark, black’ + glas ‘stream’ (a derivative of glas ‘blue’). There are several localities in Scotland and Ireland so named, but the one from which the surname is derived in most if not all cases is Douglas in Lanarkshire 20 miles south of Glasgow, the original stronghold of the influential Douglas family and their retainers.

History: The family taking their name from Douglas in Lanarkshire were of Flemish origin. They rose to great prominence in the 14th and 15th centuries, controlling the earldoms of Douglas, Morton, and Angus, and later, Queensberry.

variants.name: Douglass
meanings: Scottish and English (Durham and Northumbria): variant of   Douglas  .

History: William Douglass, a physician recognized for his identification and description of an epidemic of scarlet fever, was born c. 1691 in Gifford, Haddington County, Scotland, and settled in Boston in 1718. The abolitionist, orator, and journalist Frederick Douglass assumed the name after escaping from slavery in 1838 and traveling to Massachusetts. Son of a white father and a slave with some Indian blood, he was born Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey c. 1817 in Tuckahoe, MD.

nameMeanings.name: James
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of the name borne in the New Testament by two of Christ's disciples, James son of Zebedee and James son of Alphaeus. This form comes from Late Latin Iacomus, a variant of Iacobus, Latin form of Greek Iakobos. This is the same name as Old Testament Jacob (Hebrew Yaakov), but for many centuries now they have been thought of in the English-speaking world as two distinct names. In Britain, James is a royal name that from the beginning of the 15th century onwards was associated particularly with the Scottish house of Stewart: James I of Scotland ( 1394–1437 ; ruled 1424–37 ) was a patron of the arts and a noted poet, as well as an energetic ruler. King James VI of Scotland ( 1566–1625 ; reigned 1567–1625 ) succeeded to the throne of England in 1603 . His grandson, James II of England ( 1633–1701 ; reigned 1685–8 ) was a Roman Catholic, deposed in 1688 in favour of his Protestant daughter Mary and her husband William of Orange. From then on he, his son (also called James), and his grandson Charles (‘Bonnie Prince Charlie’) made various unsuccessful attempts to recover the English throne. Their supporters were known as Jacobites (from Latin Iacobus), and the name James became for a while particularly associated with Roman Catholicism on the one hand, and Highland opposition to the English government on the other. Nevertheless, it has since become one of the most perennially popular boys' names.

en.lifeSummary: When James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas was born in 1371, in Douglas Castle, Lanarkshire, Scotland, his father, Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwell, was 46 and his mother, Joanna Moray Countess of Douglas, was 40. He married Beatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglas in 1425, in Auchenbreck, Argyll, Scotland. They were the parents of at least 5 sons and 3 daughters. He died in 1443, in Douglas Castle, Lanarkshire, Scotland, United Kingdom, at the age of 72, and was buried in Douglas, Lanarkshire, Scotland.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas est né en 1371, à Douglas Castle, Lanarkshire, Écosse, son père, Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwell, avait 46 ans et sa mère, Joanna Moray Countess of Douglas, avait 40 ans. Il a épousé Beatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglas en 1425, à Auchenbreck, Argyll, Écosse. Ils ont eu au moins 5 fils et 3 filles. Il est décédé en 1443, à Douglas Castle, Lanarkshire, Écosse, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 72 ans, et a été enterré à Douglas, Lanarkshire, Écosse.

ja.lifeSummary: James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglasが1371年にスコットランドラナルクシュアイアドウグラス カストルで生まれた時，父Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwellは46歳，母Joanna Moray Countess of Douglasは40歳であった。1425年にスコットランドアルグイルアウチェンブレックでBeatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglasと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも5人の息子と3人の娘の親であった。彼は1443年にイギリススコットランドラナルクシュアイアドウグラス カストルで72歳で死去し，スコットランドラナルクシュアイアドウグラスにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas(이)가 1371년 스컷랜드 랜아르크실르 더글라스 카스틀에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwell(은)는 46세였고, 그의 어머니 Joanna Moray Countess of Douglas(은)는 40세였다. 그는 1425년 스컷랜드 아그이 오천브레크에서 Beatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglas(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 다섯 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1443년 영국 스코틀랜드 랜아르크실르 더글라스 카스틀에서 72세의 나이로 사망했으며 스컷랜드 랜아르크실르 더글라스에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда James Douglas 7th Earl of Douglas родился в 1371 г. в местности доуглас кастл, ланаркшир, Шотландия, его отцу по имени Archibald Douglas 3rd Earl of Douglas Earl of Wigtown Lord of Galloway and Bothwell было 46 лет, а матери, которую звали Joanna Moray Countess of Douglas, 40. Он женился на женщине по имени Beatrice Sinclair Countess of Douglas в 1425 г. в местности аучэнбрекк, аргилл, Шотландия. У них было по крайней мере 5 сыновей и 3 дочери. Он умер в 1443 г. в местности доуглас кастл, ланаркшир, Шотландия, Великобритания в возрасте 72 лет и был похоронен в местности доуглас, ланаркшир, Шотландия.
